text.title: A/B Testing Process and Metrics
text.summary: Seven-step A/B testing process with hypothesis format, metric types, sample size factors, and decision matrix.

section.title: Hypothesis Format

rule.statement: Pre-register the hypothesis, primary metric, and minimum sample size before launching.
rule.rationale: Pre-registration prevents peeking and moving goalposts; post-hoc hypothesis adjustment inflates false-positive risk.

section.p: Format: "If we [change], then [metric] will [improve/decrease] by [amount] because [reason]."

example.note: If we change the CTA from "Submit" to "Get Started Free", then click-through rate will increase by 10% because the new label communicates value rather than obligation.

section.title: Metric Types
section.p: Primary metric — the main outcome being measured (e.g., checkout completion rate). Secondary metrics — related outcomes to monitor (e.g., time on page, scroll depth). Guardrail metrics — metrics that must not worsen (e.g., revenue per session, error rate).

rule.statement: Check guardrail metrics before declaring a winner, even when the primary metric improves.
rule.rationale: A variant can win on click-through but lose on revenue; guardrails prevent shipping a net-negative change.

section.title: Sample Size Factors
section.p: Required sample size increases when: baseline conversion rate is low, desired lift (MDE) is small, statistical power is higher (80% standard), or significance level is stricter (95% standard).

rule.statement: Calculate required sample size before launch; do not end the test early because results look favorable.
rule.rationale: Stopping early on a positive trend inflates false-positive rate; the full sample is needed for valid inference.

rule.statement: Run tests for at least one full business cycle (minimum two weeks) to capture weekday/weekend variation.
rule.rationale: Traffic and conversion patterns vary by day of week; a shorter window produces biased samples.

section.title: Decision Matrix
section.p: Clear winner (statistically and practically significant) → implement variant. No difference (not significant) → keep control; simpler is better, null result is still a valid learning. Inconclusive → extend test or re-test with adjusted parameters. Negative impact on guardrail metric → stop test, investigate root cause.

rule.statement: When there is no clear winner, default to keeping the control.
rule.rationale: The control is already tested in production; the variant adds complexity without demonstrated benefit.
